title: Understanding Your Itch — and Practical Ways to Find Relief

title.value: Understanding Your Itch — and Practical Ways to Find Relief

summary.value: Intense itch is one of the most challenging parts of living with psoriasis. This article explains why it happens and shares practical, evidence-informed strategies that may help.

heroImage.altText: Itch relief illustration with shield protection icon on orange-amber gradient

body.value:
Why does psoriasis itch so much?

Itch — known medically as pruritus — is the symptom that people with psoriasis most consistently describe as their biggest daily challenge. In psoriasis, it arises from a combination of skin barrier disruption, nerve sensitisation, and inflammatory signals (particularly cytokines like IL-31) that directly activate itch-sensing nerves in the skin.

Common triggers worth knowing

  • Heat and sweating — a warm environment or physical activity raises skin temperature and can rapidly increase itch signals.
  • Stress — emotional stress activates inflammatory pathways that can worsen both plaques and itch sensation.
  • Skincare products — fragrances, sodium lauryl sulphate, and alcohol-based products can strip the skin barrier.
  • Alcohol — a known psoriasis trigger that increases skin inflammation.
  • Dry air — especially in winter or air-conditioned environments.

Breaking the scratch cycle

Scratching provides brief relief but damages the skin barrier, which drives more inflammation — and more itch. This is sometimes called the itch-scratch cycle.

  • Cool compresses — applying a cool, damp cloth temporarily damps down the nerve signals.
  • Tapping or gentle pressure — pressing the skin near an itchy patch can interrupt the itch signal.
  • Emollient application — keeping a fragrance-free moisturiser nearby can both soothe and strengthen the skin barrier.
  • Distraction — engaging activities can meaningfully reduce the perceived intensity of itch.

Clothing and your environment

Loose-fitting, soft natural fibres — cotton and bamboo in particular — minimise friction and allow skin to breathe. Managing room temperature and humidity at home is also worth attention.

This content is for educational purposes only. If your itch is severe, please speak with your dermatologist.
